industries.name: Nickel-Cadmium Batteries
uses: Nickel-cadmium (NiCd) rechargeable batteries: cadmium anode in potassium hydroxide electrolyte paired with nickel oxyhydroxide cathode — the dominant rechargeable battery chemistry for portable tools, emergency lighting, and aviation before lithium-ion

NiCd remains the preferred chemistry for aircraft emergency lighting systems and avionics backup power due to reliable performance across extreme temperatures (-40°C to +60°C) and ability to deliver high current pulses

Industrial NiCd batteries for locomotive starting, uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), and railway signaling — sintered-plate NiCd cells provide long service life (25+ years) in harsh industrial environments
NiCd has been banned in consumer portable devices in the EU since 2006 under the Battery Directive — lithium-ion has almost completely replaced NiCd in consumer electronics globally
Cordless power tools still use NiCd in price-sensitive segments and for high-drain applications, though lithium-ion is rapidly taking over even this market

companies.name: Saft (TotalEnergies)
companies.use: World's largest manufacturer of industrial NiCd batteries. Produces Saft NiCd cells for aviation emergency power, locomotive, railway signaling, and industrial UPS applications. Also produces NiMH and lithium batteries.

companies.name: EnerSys
companies.use: Major industrial battery manufacturer supplying NiCd and lead-acid batteries for motive power (forklifts), standby power (UPS, telecom), and aerospace applications. Produces Cyclon and Hawker NiCd battery systems.

industries.name: Electroplating & Pigments
uses: Cadmium electroplating on steel aerospace fasteners, landing gear components, and military hardware — cadmium provides sacrificial corrosion protection in salt spray environments and lubricates threaded fastener installation; being replaced by zinc-nickel in most applications under RoHS and REACH regulations

Cadmium sulfide (CdS) yellow and cadmium sulfoselenide (CdSₓSe₁₋ₓ) orange and red pigments: heat-stable inorganic pigments for plastics, ceramics, and glass that must withstand processing temperatures above 200°C where organic pigments would degrade

CdS and CdSe quantum dots for display technology: quantum dot-enhanced LCD displays (QLED, using cadmium-free alternatives increasingly) and quantum dot LEDs (QD-OLED) exploit size-tunable emission for wider color gamut
Cadmium telluride (CdTe) thin-film solar cells: First Solar's dominant thin-film technology uses CdTe as the p-type absorber — ~4g Cd per 100W module; accounts for ~5% of cadmium consumption globally

companies.name: First Solar
companies.share: ~5% of global solar market by GW
companies.use: World's largest CdTe thin-film solar module manufacturer. Each module uses ~4g of cadmium telluride as the semiconductor absorber layer. Operates a closed-loop cadmium recycling program, recovering CdTe from end-of-life modules.

companies.name: Ferro Corporation (Prince International)
companies.use: Major CdS and CdSe inorganic pigment producer for high-temperature plastics, ceramics, and glass applications where heat and UV stability are required. Supplies cadmium pigments to engineering plastic compounders and ceramic manufacturers.

industries.name: Nuclear Reactor Control
uses: Cadmium control rods and neutron absorbers in nuclear reactors: cadmium has a very high thermal neutron absorption cross-section (2,520 barns for ¹¹³Cd) — used in Boiling Water Reactor (BWR) control rods as Cd-In-Hf alloy
Cadmium shielding in nuclear laboratories and hot cells to filter thermal neutrons while allowing gamma rays to pass, enabling selective neutron beam experiments
Cadmium neutron capture is exploited in neutron activation analysis (NAA) to selectively irradiate epithermal neutron-sensitive samples by shielding thermal neutrons with cadmium covers

companies.name: GE-Hitachi Nuclear Energy
companies.share: ~30% of BWR nuclear fuel and components market
companies.use: Supplies BWR control rod blades containing cadmium-indium-hafnium neutron-absorbing alloy sections to boiling water reactor operators globally.
